clinical_history: This ECG from a middle-aged woman is most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnoses?
diagnosis: This ECG shows prominent resting sinus tachycardia (at 120/min) with PR prolongation, especially for this sinus rate (210 ms). The most notable findings are right atrial abnormality (tall, peaked P waves in leads II and V1), markedly rightward QRS axis deviation in the frontal plane (about +120 degrees), relatively tall R waves in V1-V2 and T wave inversions  in V1-V3. This constellation of findings is virtually diagnostic of a severe right heart overload syndrome. In this case, the patient had chronic pulmonary hypertension (multifactorial), including severe restrictive lung disease.~~~~Note that a relatively tall R wave in lead V1 is an ECG marker of posterior infarction (equivalent to a pathologic Q wave in posterior leads, which are not generally recorded). However, posterior infarction would not account for the ensemble of findings, also including right axis deviation of the QRS, peaked P waves consistent with right atrial abnormality and ST-T wave abnormalities, consistent with right ventricular overload. Furthermore, what is called "posterior MI" by ECG is usually associated with concomitant evidence of inferior and/or lateral wall involvement.
